title: From Control To Surrender
study: A Study In The Life Of King David
scriptureForWeek: 1 SAMUEL 18-21 The theme of these chapters is the tension of loneliness and friendship,especially as a leader and soon to be king.
weekTheme: Boys isolate, men connect God will give us brothers There is an epidemic of loneliness among young men today. It’s hard to build true friendships in the world, but even harder for younger guys to connect without feeling weird. There are a lot of surface-level connections, like online gaming or sports teams, but it’s hard to be vulnerable and open up. In a 2021 survey of 2,000 men in the United States, 61% reported feeling lonely at least sometimes, with 25% saying they feel lonely often or all of the time. These are really shocking numbers. The reasons for this are pretty complex. Social anxiety: With so much time spent online and social isolation during the pandemic, young guys can feel fear or anxiety about entering new environments and meeting new people. Lack of social skills: Some guys may not have yet developed the necessary social skills to make and maintain friendships, such as active listening, communication, and handling conflict. Shared connections and interests: Young guys who have different interests or values than their peers may struggle to find others who share their faith, passions, or convictions. Technology and social media: While technology and social media have made it easier to connect in some ways, it can also create a sense of isolation and lead to a lack of face- to-face interaction. Most relationships are mediated through screens. Guys today do a ton of stuff online but can struggle to figure out how to translate that to face to face friendships. You have probably seen a group of teenagers at the mall or a coffee shop, all looking at their phones and texting each other, but not being willing to say things face to face. These communication dynamics are hard to work through. This week, David is going to teach us about how to surrender our loneliness to God and rely on the friendships and relationships He brings us. This will happen through looking

days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 18:1-4 After David had finished talking with Saul, he met Jonathan, the king’s son. There was an immediate bond between them, for Jonathan loved David. From that day on Saul kept David with him and wouldn’t let him return home. And Jonathan made a solemn pact with David, because he loved him as he loved himself. Jonathan sealed the pact by taking off his robe and giving it to David, together with his tunic, sword, bow, and belt.
days.reflection: Making new friends can be hard. It can be embarrassing and awkward. You can feel super self-conscious, insecure, and worried about being rejected. This can lead to loneliness, anxiety, and fear. Have you ever experienced that? However, when you do meet someone you really connect with, it can be the coolest thing ever. To really gel with someone and realize you like the same things and are heading on the same path is one of the deepest joys a guy your age can have. C.S. Lewis (the man who wrote the Narnia series) once said, "Friendship is born at the moment when one person says to another: 'What! You too? I thought I was the only one.'" Loneliness is thinking you are the only one. David knew about this loneliness. Have you ever thought about how lonely he must have been during his teenage years? His Dad didn’t honor him and left him looking after the sheep in the field. He didn’t even bother to call him in when Samuel the prophet came to anoint a new king. David’s brothers didn’t like him, and they criticized and rebuked him when he came to fight Goliath. King Saul was jealous of him because of his victory over Goliath. David must have felt deep loneliness, which is probably why he ended up becoming such a good songwriter and man of prayer. He had no one else to talk to! But then, he met Jonathan and everything changed. Jonathan was Saul’s son and the rightful heir to the throne. Yet, his friendship with David would change his life, too, in ways he didn’t know. And this friendship was deep. Look at the intensity of the language. “There was an immediate bond. He loved him as himself.” This isn’t just having a few shared interests. This is brotherly love. As you grow up and become a man, you are going to have to learn to surrender your loneliness and friendships to God. You are going to have to ask Him for comfort when you are alone and friends for the journey. Deuteronomy 31:8 says, “Do not be afraid or discouraged, for the LORD will personally go ahead of you. He will be with you; He will neither fail you nor abandon you.” God will be with us as we learn to go look for other guys to do life with. As you grow up, you are going to have to learn a ton of new lessons to thrive as a man. One of the most important ones will be about friendships. Muhammad Ali, the famous boxer, once said, "Friendship is the hardest thing in the world to explain. It's not something you learn in school. But if you haven't learned the meaning of friendship, you really haven't learned anything." There are a lot of smart, rich, and famous lonely men. You don’t want to be one of them. Offer your loneliness to God. You will be amazed at how He meets you and the friends He brings you way.
questions: In general, do you think guys your age get lonely that often? Why or why not?
On a scale of 1 to 10, how lonely would you say you feel most of the time? How lonely would you say you feel right now?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they dealt with loneliness and also about how they met one of their closest friends.

thoughts.verse: There are “friends” who destroy each other, but a real friend sticks closer than a brother.
prompts: -Is there something that stands out to you in it?
-Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
-Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to share any feelings of loneliness you may have with God. Ask Him for His comfort and love.

days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 18:1-4 After David had finished talking with Saul, he met Jonathan, the king’s son. There was an immediate bond between them, for Jonathan loved David. From that day on Saul kept David with him and wouldn’t let him return home. And Jonathan made a solemn pact with David, because he loved him as he loved himself. Jonathan sealed the pact by taking off his robe and giving it to David, together with his tunic, sword, bow, and belt.
days.reflection: We live in a consumer society. You have probably heard that term before but may not be aware of how it impacts our relationships. A consumer society is based on getting the most value for the lowest price. People today are always looking for a “deal!” They ask, “Is the price worth it?” “Is there enough value here for me to buy this?” “Can I get more value for less money?” It's okay to try to get the best price when it comes to buying a new TV, but it’s bad to think like that about friendships. Consumerism applied to relationships does so much damage. Guys your age may talk about this using simpler language. They probably talk about people “using someone.” They may say things like, “He was only using her to make out with her,” or “he was using him to try to get on the team.” This idea of using people for our own benefit is not just selfish, it’s tragic. Using people creates heartache, suspicion, and tons of pain. The opposite of using people is loving them. Unlike what the world thinks, love isn’t really about a feeling; it’s about a commitment. We show people we care about them by being loyal to them. In this passage today, we see that David and Jonathan really cared about each other as friends and were deeply committed to one another. Jonathan made a solemn pact with David because he loved him as he loved himself. Jonathan sealed the pact by taking off his robe and giving it to David with his tunic, sword, bow, and belt. The pact they made is called a covenant. It’s a really serious commitment. Guys today make this kind of commitment when they join a fraternity at college or join an accountability group at church. It’s a promise to build your friendship on commitment, not just convenience. To really be there when people need you. To show up for someone when they are in trouble and need help, whether you feel like it or not. It’s kind of like Sam’s commitment to Frodo in Lord of the Rings. Sam was committed to walk with Frodo wherever it took him or whatever it cost him. From the Shire to Mordor, Sam was loyal in spite of danger, trials, and pain. His commitment went way beyond that of personal convenience. In one powerful moment, he says, "I made a promise, Mr. Frodo. A promise. "Don't you leave him, Samwise Gamgee." And I don't mean to." And he didn’t. If you are going to be a true friend, you will have to surrender your desire for convenience, your need to get your own way, and your selfishness. It will be worth it in the end because the best relationships are ones of commitment, not convenience. And your commitment in your relationships is built on Jesus’ commitment to you. Jesus is loyal to us when we sin, when we struggle, when we doubt, and when we fail. John 15:13 says, “There is no greater love than to lay down one’s life for one’s friends.” Jesus laid down His life for us, and He calls us to lay down our lives for each other. Boys build friendships on convenience; men build friendships on commitment. Resolve in your heart to be a man of commitment today.
questions: Have you ever felt like someone has used you before? If so, what was it and how did it make you feel? If not, be grateful.
Do you have any friends in your life that are really important to you like a really close pal? How do you know that your friendships are strong and committed?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share about a friend they are really committed to. How does that commitment show up in real life? Be specific.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Two people are better off than one, for they can help each other succeed. If one person falls, the other can reach out and help. But someone who falls alone is in real trouble. Likewise, two people lying close together can keep each other warm. But how can one be warm alone? A person standing alone can be attacked and defeated, but two can stand back-to-back and conquer.
prompts: -Is there something that stands out to you in it?
-Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
-Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you some godly friends. Ask for a couple of guys you can really go deep with and be honest with. If you have them already, pray that God would bless and protect your friendship.

days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 20:1-17 David now fled from Naioth in Ramah and found Jonathan. “What have I done?” he exclaimed. “What is my crime? How have I offended your father that he is so determined to kill me?” “That’s not true!” Jonathan protested. “You’re not going to die. He always tells me everything he’s going to do, even the little things. I know my father wouldn’t hide something like this from me. It just isn’t so!” Then David took an oath before Jonathan and said, “Your father knows perfectly well about our friendship, so he has said to himself, ‘I won’t tell Jonathan—why should I hurt him?’ But I swear to you that I am only a step away from death! I swear it by the LORD and by your own soul!” “Tell me what I can do to help you,” Jonathan exclaimed. David replied, “Tomorrow we celebrate the new moon festival. I’ve always eaten with the king on this occasion, but tomorrow I’ll hide in the field and stay there until the evening of the third day. If your father asks where I am, tell him I asked permission to go home to Bethlehem for an annual family sacrifice. If he says, ‘Fine!’ you will know all is well. But if he is angry and loses his temper, you will know he is determined to kill me. Show me this loyalty as my sworn friend—for we made a solemn pact before the LORD—or kill me yourself if I have sinned against your father. But please don’t betray me to him!” “Never!” Jonathan exclaimed. “You know that if I had the slightest notion my father was planning to kill you, I would tell you at once.” Then David asked, “How will I know whether or not your father is angry?” “Come out to the field with me,” Jonathan replied. And they went out there together. Then Jonathan told David, “I promise by the LORD, the God of Israel, that by this time tomorrow, or the next day at the latest, I will talk to my father and let you know at once how he feels about you. If he speaks favorably about you, I will let you know. 13 But if he is angry and wants you killed, may the LORD strike me and even kill me if I don’t warn you so you can escape and live. May the LORD be with you as he used to be with my father. And may you treat me with the faithful love of the LORD as long as I live. But if I die, treat my family with this faithful love, even when the LORD destroys all your enemies from the face of the earth.” So Jonathan made a solemn pact with David, saying, “May the LORD destroy all your enemies!” And Jonathan made David reaffirm his vow of friendship again, for Jonathan loved David as he loved himself.
days.reflection: Who do you trust? Which of your friends can you really count on? Who do you know that will tell you the truth and be honest with you, even if you don’t like what you hear? We need trustworthy friends who will tell us what is really happening around us. One of the best things you can do as a friend is learn to tell the truth. But this isn’t easy. We live in a world where people would rather be liked than be honest. So many guys today shrink back from being honest because they are worried about being rejected or bringing conflict into a friendship. This is actually kind of selfish. True friends tell the truth, even if it’s hard for the other person to hear. Many leadership failures happen today because people surround themselves with guys who just tell them what they want to hear. These kinds of men aren’t real friends; they are called “yes” men. Have you heard that phrase before? A “yes” man is someone who does whatever the other person wants even if they disagree with it, think it's harmful, or a dumb idea. Being loyal isn’t always about saying yes, it’s about telling the truth no matter the cost. Be a truth teller, not a “yes” man. A “yes” man... Loves himself more than his friend. Holds back information out of fear. Goes along with dumb ideas because he doesn’t want to be rejected. Can't live with any kind of disagreement. Is a Muppet and not a man. A true man... Puts his friends’ well-being first. Lovingly shares hard words of truth. Speaks up when dumb ideas are shared. Appeals to his friend to do the right thing out of love. Is not a Muppet and has real courage. In today’s reading, David was worried that King Saul was trying to kill him, and it was filling him with fear. He didn’t know who he could trust in the palace or who would tell him what was really going on. So, he talked to his friend Jonathan to find out what was really happening. Jonathan was willing to find out the truth out of love for his friend, even if he through he was being paranoid. So he devised a way to see his Dad’s motives and was willing to share a hard word, even when it caused David great pain. Their friendship was built on trust, and trust comes from telling the truth no matter what. Ephesians 4:15 talks about “Speaking the truth in love.” This is what real friends do. Some guys speak the truth, but they are harsh and mean. Some guys are loving but hold the truth back out of fear. But truth and love together build a deep sense of trust between friends that lets them prosper. Jesus modeled this so well. He told us the truth about our sin and brokenness and warned against the dangers of hell yet loved us enough to die for our sin and save us from it all. Truth and love are the foundation of good friendships. Jonathan and David show us this, and Jesus modeled it. Be a true man, and not a “yes” man, and you will be a friend others can count on.
questions: How do you handle sharing hard things with other guys?
What is the hardest truth anyone has ever said to you?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share about a time when a friend of theirs shared something that was true but hard to hear. What was it, and how did they see this as a form of kindness.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verse slowly. Better is open rebuke than hidden love. Wounds from a friend can be trusted, but an enemy multiplies kisses.
prompts: -Is there something that stands out to you in it?
-Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
-Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that He has told you the truth about your brokenness and your worth. Ask Him for help and guidance for any situation you are facing right now.

days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 20:30-33 Saul boiled with rage at Jonathan. “You stupid son of a whore!” he swore at him. “Do you think I don’t know that you want him to be king in your place, shaming yourself and your mother? As long as that son of Jesse is alive, you’ll never be king. Now go and get him so I can kill him!” “But why should he be put to death?” Jonathan asked his father. “What has he done?” Then Saul hurled his spear at Jonathan, intending to kill him. So at last Jonathan realized that his father was really determined to kill David.
days.reflection: What do you think the most important trait is in a friendship? Take a moment and think carefully. Is it having fun? Having the same hobbies? Liking the same kinds of video games? No. It’s deeper than all these things. It’s being loyal. All good men stand with their friends when their friends face opposition. They stick by their side no matter what. Don’t ever be like Judas who betrayed Jesus when things got hard, just so he could get some money. Selling your friends out is never worth it. Sticking with someone through thick and thin is called being loyal. It means that you are committed to someone and will not be swayed even if you face resistance and opposition for this friendship. In today’s passage, we see Jonathan’s loyalty to his friend David. Jonathan knew that God had anointed David to be the king. But this meant that even though he held the right to the throne in the normal order of things, he was willing to sacrifice and give it up in alignment with God’s plans. Jonathan’s loyalty wasn’t just an idea, it was a reality. You also see how his loyalty to God and David was tested when he came into conflict with his father, King Saul. Remember back to week 1? Saul was a control freak. He wouldn’t trust God or surrender to his plans; he wanted to do things his own way. Now Saul turns his frustrations on his own son. He calls him some pretty strong language and tries to kill him by throwing a spear at him. Imagine that for a Dad? Jonathan had a choice. Do what his godless Father wanted or be loyal to his friend, the one God had chosen. He made the right choice: loyalty to David. Jonathan’s loyalty is a picture of God’s loyalty and love to us. You may remember that one of the words for God’s love in the Old Testament is the word Hesed. It’s a strong word that means deeply committed love. It’s translated as loving-kindness or faithful love. And that kind of love is the standard and model for all human love. We need to learn to be loyal in our friendships. So often, guys abandon their friends when conflict comes, or it costs them acceptance and affirmation from a larger group of guys. We must surrender our need to be loved by the world to be loyal to the people God has given us. Jesus was and is even more loyal than Jonathan. He was faithful to His heavenly Father and His friends, even though they betrayed Him, denied Him, and abandoned Him when things got hard. When He rose from the dead, He didn’t go and fight Caesar in Rome or confront the religious leaders who plotted against Him. He went and restored Peter, who had gone back to fishing, showed Thomas His hands and sides to ease his doubts, and appeared to the disciples on the road to Emmaus, who were walking away from Him. He went and found all His friends who got lost along the way. Jesus used His resurrection power not for His own revenge, but to restore His friends. Be like Jonathan; be like Jesus. Learn to be loyal, the best kind of friend.
questions: Can you think of a moment when you have loyal to one of your friends when it was kind of hard to do so? What happened, and what did you do?
Who is the most loyal friend you have? How do they show this to you?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share about a loyal friend they have. How have they shown this over the years? Give a few clear examples.

thoughts.verse: There are “friends” who destroy each other, but a real friend sticks closer than a brother.
prompts: -Is there something that stands out to you in it?
-Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
-Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for being so loyal to you. Ask Him for power to be loyal to your friends and anything they are going through right now.

days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL23:15-18 One day near Horesh, David received the news that Saul was on the way to Ziph to search for him and kill him. Jonathan went to find David and encouraged him to stay strong in his faith in God. “Don’t be afraid,” Jonathan reassured him. “My father will never find you! You are going to be the king of Israel, and I will be next to you, as my father, Saul, is well aware.” So the two of them renewed their solemn pact before the LORD. Then Jonathan returned home, while David stayed at Horesh.
days.reflection: People in our world today are so critical. Everyone thinks they are entitled to say whatever they want, whenever they want. Social media has made this worse, and so have phones. People say in text messages or comments what they would never say to someone in real life. This is making people more mean and more cowardly at the same time. At some point, this kind of criticism is going to come at you. There are so many things that you will face in life that will beat you down. You will face complaints, opposition, confusion, and resistance that will make you want to give up and quit. Learning to handle discouragement is important as you grow into a man, but there is something just as important as that--learning to become an encourager. There is something so powerful about friends who come along side us to encourage us. When you are beat down and a friend speaks kind words to you and seeks to cheer you up, it’s one of the most amazing things that can happen in your life. It can totally lift your spirits. You feel like a new person, you feel like you can keep going, and you feel like things may turn out ok after all. Yet, so many people tear others down with their words. Resolve in your heart to use your words to build others up. Paul said in 1 Thessalonians 5:11, “So encourage each other and build each other up, just as you are already doing.” This is what Jonathan did when David was beaten down. David was hiding out of fear of his life, with King Saul chasing him down to kill him. Jonathan spoke life and hope into his heart, which built him up. Look at how specific Jonathan’s encouragement was. It wasn’t just hype or random encouragement. It was real, specific, and timely. He said... Don’t be afraid. He addressed David’s fears and reassured him of God’s call. You will be king. He reminded David of God’s promises for his future and the certainty of his destiny. I will be next to you. We will do some cool stuff together when you are in power. This encouragement gave David so much hope and enabled him to keep going. Proverbs 27:9 says, “The heartfelt counsel of a friend is as sweet as perfume and incense.” Similarly, elsewhere in Proverbs, “The words of the wise bring healing.” This week, you have seen how all men will wrestle with loneliness and discouragement in their lives. You will too. You have to learn to surrender this to God and trust Him to give you the relationships that you need. God provided a best friend in Jonathan, and He wants to make sure you find some guys you can really connect with and do life with, too. Wherever you are now in terms of your friends, lay it before God, and invite Him to lead and guide you. You are going to need a crew to make it through. Build your crew wisely.
questions: Can you think of a situation in your life recently where you were beaten down, and someone came alongside you to encourage you? Who was it, and how did it help?
Is there someone in your life you right now that you can think of that seems a bit discouraged or is struggling? What could you do this week to build them up.
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share who his most encouraging friend is and why. How did he first meet this person, and how did he build this friendship.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Let us hold unswervingly to the hope we profess, for he who promised is faithful. And let us consider how we may spur one another on toward love and good deeds, not giving up meeting together, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging one another —and all the more as you see the Day approaching.
prompts: -Is there something that stands out to you in it?
-Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
-Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to pray for one of your friends that needs encouragement. Ask God to strengthen them and help them. You may want to shoot them an email or text as well to let them know you are thinking of them.

extras: WEEK 4 MANSCHOOL KNIFE SHARPENING MAN SCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. -John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: FEAR FACTOR In men’s ministries, people throw Proverbs 27:17 around a lot. “As iron sharpens iron, so a friend sharpens a friend.” This week, get a new knife—something cool (James Brand is my favorite), but anything will do. Cut various materials when it's sharp to test its blade. Then cut a bunch of metal and rock to blunt it. Then, get a sharpener and teach him how to sharpen the knife. It would be ideal if you had a full-blown blacksmith's kiln, but alas, a knife will do. Then, talk about how knife sharpening works and the importance of friendships sharpening us. OPTION 2. WATCH The Sandlot The Sandlot Kids. Absolute classic. Friendships formed, and a sense of belonging. Good heading into summer, too. OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son out to one of his favorite restaurants, or for desserts and talk about loneliness and friendships. There is a good chance that your son is lonelier than you know and may be facing real relational conflicts he struggles to articulate. Talk to him about staying committed when other guys bail. Talk to him about being loyal but being wise. Talk to him about being a “yes” man vs a “true” man. Tell him you know how hard it is and reassure him with your life.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
